data.title: VanEck Crypto Monthly Recap For November 2025
data.description:
Price and onchain metrics weakened, but institutional participation and lower volatility point to a milder drawdown than last cycle’s -78% decline.

Please note that VanEck may have a position(s) in the digital asset(s) described below.

Crypto investors felt like they were tossed through a washing machine in November, as relentless selling pressure shook coins loose from weaker hands. The result was that Bitcoin (BTC-USD) reached the lowest 30-day Relative Strength Index (RSI) readings (~32) since the de-peg of Lido ETH in June 2022. RSI measures the speed and magnitude of recent price changes to identify overbought or oversold conditions. BTC, ETH, and SOL (SOL-USD) each fell (-23%), (-27%), and (-31%). Crypto selloffs were heavily concentrated during US trading sessions, which contributed roughly 85% of November BTC losses.

Though the Coinbase Premium Index, which tracks Coinbase’s BTC price premium to non-US exchanges, briefly turned positive over Thanksgiving weekend, the index was negative for the vast majority of November. The “flush” in November 2025 somewhat mirrors the one crypto experienced after Trump’s tariffs in April 2025, when BTC fell from $109K to $76K. The result of recent price action is that (~55%) of Bitcoin’s supply is in profit, which marks the lowest reading since September 2023. In the previous bear market, Bitcoin supply in profit reached a low of (~31%) in November 2022 after the collapse of FTX.

MarketVector Smart Contract Leaders Index (MVSCLE) Fell by -25% in November

Open interest in crypto futures continued to decline from October, reaching the lowest levels, $29B, since the chaos of the tariff market reaction in April 2025. BTC ETP outflows in BTC terms were (-2.5%), while ETH ETP redemptions corresponded to (-8%) of AUM measured in ETH. The extreme levels of uncertainty drove 30-day trailing volatility to the mid-40s, approaching the lower levels seen in April 2025, when volatility averaged above 50. Besides broader concerns about AI spending and Federal Reserve policy, crypto traders worried about emerging narratives around quantum computing affecting Bitcoin encryption, Digital Asset Treasury (DAT) weakness, and selling by ancient whales.

Cumulative BTC Returns by Session 10/24 - 11/24

This dismal backdrop translated into weak onchain fundamentals, with blockchain revenues down (-37%) m/m to reach ~$200M in November. DEX volumes across all chains were down (-26%) m/m and (-35%) y/y. Meanwhile, Hyperliquid gained more market share of blockchain earnings, reaching (40%) of the market to gross $80M on the month. After the conclusion of the perpetual future DEX mania of October, BNB’s revenues have fallen (-76%) m/m, dropping it to fifth place in blockchain revenues. Stablecoin (SBC-USD) transfer volumes were down (-19%) m/m across all chains, but still showed y/y growth of (+54%). In that category, Ethereum (ETH-USD) continues to dominate, posting roughly the same volumes as the next four competitors combined.

Hyperliquid Revenue Gains as BNB Fades

Stablecoins on blockchain also pulled back after reaching an all-time high of 308B in October, declining to 304B at the end of November. Though USDC lost (-2%) or 1.2B of its market capitalization, Ethena’s USDE lost (-30%) m/m or nearly (-50%) off its mid-October high (14B-$7B). The decline in overall stablecoins partly relates to the compressing rates environment for crypto. The best gauge of blockchain yields, the trailing 30-day perp funding rates, fell as low as (3.8%) after averaging (7-8%) over the summer. These yields reflect crypto traders’ willingness to take directional risk, and current levels are the lowest since October 2023. With respect to Ethena, the substantial decline is most likely attributable to the peg break it experienced due to market chaos on October 10, when some exchanges priced USDE as low as $0.65 rather than $1.00. Another bit of bad news for stablecoins came at the end of the month when S&P downgraded Tether’s rating for peg stability from 4 (constrained) to 5 (weak), which is S&P’s lowest rating.

As an ending note, we would like to remember one of the best F1 drivers of all time, Ayrton Senna. Ayrton relished rainy race days because wet conditions amplified his bold, precise skills, allowing him to overcome substantial deficits to win races. When other drivers dialed back their risk tolerances, Ayrton increased his and benefited enormously. Chaotic times offer immense opportunities for the disciplined investor. While we do not know when this downpour will end, we are carefully positioning our portfolios for the sunny times to come.

30-Day Correlation BTC/Nasdaq Broke 1-Year Highs

Top Crypto Questions from Clients This Month

1 - Are bitcoin whales selling?

Bitcoin Spent Volume from 5+ Year Holders (30-Day Moving Average)

We have seen a modest uptick in selling of older cohorts of BTC holdings. As we can see in the chart above, this selling by older cohorts is far below earlier peaks. The largest reductions in positions have occurred in the 2-5-year holding band. This cohort has dropped in total BTC holdings by (-31%) since November 2023. These holders appear to be positioning based on their interpretation of Bitcoin’s place in the 4-year cycle. Zooming out, while the percentage of supply >5 years old has remained consistent at (30.5%), we have seen a massive drop in % the supply >2 years old category, moving from (57%) in January 2024 to (48%) today. All the while, short-term active supply, <6M Old, has surged from (22%) in June 2025 to (31%) today, which is the highest level since July 2021.

2 - Is it too late to buy bitcoin?

In our opinion, it is difficult to call any level ‘too late’ for BTC, and these levels may reflect short-term panic more than a long-term view of fundamental potential. BTC recently experienced a greater than (-30%) drawdown, which mirrors past pullbacks during bull markets in 2017 and 2021.

3 - Is crypto in a bubble?

Some crypto tokens certainly need to be repriced, including those associated with ghost blockchains and low-utility applications. However, we believe there are still worthwhile investment opportunities for those with a very high risk tolerance.

4 - Have we passed the price peak in Bitcoin's 4-year cycle?

Many 4-year cycle forecasters divine that we have already reached the price apex of this cycle. However, lower volatility and the explosion in real-money involvement suggest a smaller drawdown than previous cycles. Thus, VanEck bought the dip in model portfolios at ~$80K on Friday, November 21st, seeing an attractive risk/reward, with technical indicators signaling a near-term bottom. However, we remain flexible and not dogmatic about the cycle with a healthy respect for the 4-year patterns.

The underlying dynamics of the 4-year cycle may have changed as Bitcoin miners diversified their income streams and institutional players viewed BTC as a unique macro asset. That noted, many in the crypto community, including large whales, believe that the Bitcoin 4-year cycle is infallible. If these parties act, they will likely weigh on prices.

Index Definitions

Nasdaq 100 Index: is comprised of 100 of the largest and most innovative non-financial companies listed on the Nasdaq Stock Market based on market capitalization.

MarketVector Meme Coin Index: modified market cap-weighted index which tracks the performance of the 6 largest meme coins. Meme coin refers to crypto assets often named after characters, individuals, animals, artworks, or other memetic elements. Initially supported by enthusiastic online traders and communities, these coins are intended for entertainment purposes.

Coin Definitions

Bitcoin (BTC): A decentralized digital currency enabling peer-to-peer transactions without intermediaries or a central authority.

Ethereum (ETH): A decentralized smart-contract platform used to build and run applications and Layer-2 networks.

Solana (SOL): A high-throughput Layer-1 blockchain; SOL is used for fees and staking to secure the network.

BNB (BNB): The native asset of BNB Chain used for transaction fees, staking, and ecosystem utilities.

USD Coin (USDC): A fiat-backed U.S.-dollar stablecoin issued by regulated partners, designed to maintain a 1:1 USD peg.

Tether (USDT): A widely used fiat-backed U.S.-dollar stablecoin intended to hold a 1:1 USD peg.

Ethena USDe (USDE): A synthetic U.S.-dollar stablecoin from Ethena that targets a ~$1 value via a delta-neutral backing model; market price can trade above/below the peg during stress.

Lido Staked Ether (stETH): A liquid staking token representing staked ETH on Lido that accrues staking rewards and is redeemable for ETH over time.

Hyperliquid (HYPE): The native token of the Hyperliquid L1 + perps DEX, used for governance, staking/fee-sharing, and protocol incentives.

data.title: 2026: A Rare Alignment Of Policy, Liquidity, And AI
data.description:
Thesis Summary

We're less than 30 days away from 2026, and we are entering the year with a macro setup unlike anything we've seen in years.

President Trump has confirmed he will announce his new Federal Reserve Chair "early next year."

Treasury Secretary Bessent has stated that "substantial tax refunds" are coming in Q1 2026.

Together with rate cuts, other forms of monetary and fiscal stimulus, and the acceleration in the AI spending cycle, you have a recipe for a good year in stocks.

The message for investors has been clear since the day Trump got elected. Investors need to own assets.

2026 is shaping up to be the year policy, liquidity, and sentiment finally align.

A New Fed Chair and a New Regime

Trump's comment that his new Fed Chair will be announced "early next year" is one of the most important macro signals of the year.

Odds are strongly in favour of Kevin Hassett.

But what matters is that a Trump-appointed chair is overwhelmingly likely to prioritise:

  • Lower rates

  • A more dovish stance on inflation

  • Full employment and growth over restrictive policy

  • Coordination with fiscal authorities rather than conflict

Even before a new chair takes over, markets will begin pricing an easier Fed. That alone could be enough to shift behaviour across credit, equities, crypto, and risk assets.

Not Just Rate Cuts

Finally, the Supplementary Leverage Ratio (SLR) has been modified. Behold, the eSLR.

Regulators have signed off on a long-sought easing of capital rules for the largest U.S. banks, cutting the extra leverage cushion tied to Treasury holdings in a move meant to free up balance sheets and encourage more government bond buying.

The change centers on the enhanced supplementary leverage ratio or eSLR and reflects a Trump administration push to dial back post-crisis constraints and draw more bank demand toward longer-term Treasurys such as the 10-year note.

Source: Globest.com

While this has largely gone unnoticed, it's a policy that I flagged months ago and something that should go a long way to ease capital and help lower yields.

Q1 2026: Treasury-Driven Liquidity Wave

Treasury Secretary Bessent's comment that "substantial tax refunds" are coming in Q1 should be well-received by the market

Refund season is already one of the largest natural liquidity injections of the year. In 2026, that injection is expected to be earlier and larger

Refunds hitting household accounts in Q1 function exactly like stimulus.

They increase spending, raise consumption, and improve the earnings backdrop for corporates.

They also drive immediate flows into markets.

Q1 often sets the tone for the entire year, and a liquidity-positive Q1 historically leads to double-digit annual equity performance.

Stimulus Checks Are Not Off the Table

While nothing formal has been announced, the political incentive is powerful.

Combine high interest costs, a high cost of living, and an administration that seeks to deliver visible wins early, and the probability of targeted stimulus payments rises significantly.

Even modest stimulus checks can meaningfully influence:

  • Consumer confidence

  • Retail activity

  • Corporate revenue numbers

  • Market sentiment

  • Fund flows

A similar, though perhaps smaller, dynamic in 2026 could supercharge risk assets at a time when AI, semiconductors, and infrastructure spending are already in full expansion mode.

The AI Supercycle Adds Fuel

The global AI build-out is continuing at historic speed.

Hyperscalers are committing tens of billions per quarter in capex, and the industry is entering the inference and commercial deployment phase.

And again, the Trump admin has a hand in here, something I talked about in my last piece on the AI bubble.

The Genesis Mission will set the government up to be a big player in AI, providing not just a backstop, but also fueling investment and animal spirits.

In a year when policy is easing and fiscal flows are rising, AI acts as the structural tailwind that supports higher multiples.

2026 will be the first full year when both AI and liquidity move in the same direction.

How Should You Invest?

So, which are the sectors best positioned to benefit from the 2026 macro regime:

1. Mega-Cap Tech & AI Platforms

Of course, mega-caps are still poised to outperform. Not just because of their exposure to AI, but also, importantly, because they make a substantial amount of revenue from abroad, which also makes them a great dollar hedge.

2. AI Infrastructure & Data Centers

Though people talk of a bubble, I think we are early in the cycle, with credit easing and the government stepping in to further help these companies.

3. Bitcoin & Crypto

Historically, Bitcoin follows liquidity with near-perfect correlation. Rate cuts and refunds alone are bullish. Add the potential for stimulus, and it becomes rocket fuel.

True, this doesn't play out with the four-year cycle, but this hasn't played out the way it was supposed to.

Risks

No macro environment is without risk.

Unexpected inflation re-acceleration would be a big problem for the rate cut narrative.

There's also a risk of global shocks (energy, geopolitics, or supply chain disruptions)

And though most believe the Fed and Trump will now align, there's room for some more drama in this narrative, which could shake markets

Final Thoughts

The coming year presents an alignment that investors rarely get:

  • A new Fed chair

  • Rate cuts likely

  • A massive Q1 liquidity injection from tax refunds

  • Potential stimulus on the horizon

  • Ongoing fiscal expansion

  • An AI capital spending supercycle

  • Improving global liquidity conditions

For long-term investors, the message is simple. Keep adding, and make sure you own assets. As always, the way I see it, the bigger risk here is staying in cash.

data.title: RingCentral: The Operating Leverage Effect Still At Work
data.description:
Since our last review of RingCentral's (RNG) earnings report, the company's shares have fallen by 4% as the S&P 500 has risen by 7%. To be honest, we have not found any clear prerequisites for such a dynamic of quotations relative to the market. However, we believe that all of our key arguments remain valid. The company continues to ramp up its profitability, which could lead to a revaluation of its stock in the future.

3Q earnings

- Revenue climbed by 5% y/y to $638.7 mln (~$3 mln above consensus);

- EBITDA jumped by 50.7% y/y to $87.2 mln. We calculate EBITDA as operating income + D&A;

- Subscription revenue went up by 6% y/y to $616 mln

- ARR totaled $2,630 mln (+6% y/y);

- The number of AI Receptionist users rocketed by 85% q/q to 5,800+ clients;

- RingCX (an AI-powered contact center solution) has been in place for as long as a year, with the number of customers rising by 150% to more than 1,350 over the time;

- The AI Conversation Expert (the new name for RingSense) expanded by 250% y/y to 4300+ customers;

- Customer retention holds at about 99%. We estimate that ARR retention reached 99.3%, an increase from 98.7% in 2Q.

Management’s guidance

The guidance for revenue growth in 2025 has been tweaked to the downside, or from 4%-6% to 4.5%-5%. But the FCF forecast has been raised again, from $515-$520 mln to $525-$530 mln, which at the moment translates into a FWD FCF Yield of ~19.5%. GAAP operating margin is expected to reach 4.8%-5% in 2025.

Interpreting the earnings report

The report slightly beat expectations, but the shares fell at the time of its publication, probably due to a general market correction, along with the downward revision of the 2025 revenue range in the guidance. However, the shares subsequently retraced some of the losses. It should be noted that subscription revenue is growing at a normal rate of 6% per year. The slowdown is in “other income,” which is not segmented affects about 3% of total revenue.

On the plus side, following a consistent slowdown in the growth of subscription revenue (which spanned three straight quarters), we are finally seeing an acceleration, albeit a slight one (from 5.6% y/y in 2Q to 5.7% y/yin 3Q). The company has probably reached a plateau, and we believe the slowdown will halt in the future, with the pace of growth stabilizing.

The company introduced a new product, AVA (AI Virtual Assistant). It is an agent that helps optimize and automate tasks during customer interactions. RingSense was also renamed AI Conversation Expert (ACE). Management emphasized that these products are already beginning to make a significant contribution. The target for AI products remains the same: $100 mln in ARR by the end of 2025. According to the earnings call transcript, CEO Vladimir Shmunis announced the company's transition to RingCentral 3.0, or a new era of agentic voice AI. The company is evolving from a UCaaS provider to a multi-platform communications provider, where AI agents are integrated at all stages of customer interaction.

RingCentral is strengthening its position in healthcare, finance, retail, and professional services. Voice traffic on the platform is growing at double-digit rates, with users making tens of millions of calls per day (~100 mln minutes), reaffirming the stability of the core voice business. Joint sales with GSP partners (operators), primarily AT&T, are picking up speed: the GSP channel already makes up more than 10% of total revenue and is expanding at a double-digit pace, on track to achieve operating profitability in less than 18 months.

Meanwhile, the company's profitability is improving at a rapid clip. As a reminder, our main pitch for RNG was not about astronomical revenue growth but operating leverage. We are currently seeing this in the form of a 1.5x y/y increase in EBITDA, as well as a second straight quarter of positive GAAP net income. RingCentral also reported positive earnings for the first nine months of 2025 ($20.4 mln).

Optimization of expenses as a percentage of revenue, as well as working capital management (primarily reducing accounts receivable and managing prepaid/deferred expenses for sales agent commissions), has had a positive impact on cash flows: The company has raised its FCF forecast in each quarter of the current financial year.

That means the company continues to ramp up profitability as its revenue growth stabilizes. The subscription segment is expected to deliver a growth of 5.5%-6%. We believe that the business is developing in a positive direction overall.

Additionally, as part of our model:

- We see a positive trend in ARR retention (we define this indicator as annualized revenue for the current quarter divided by ARR for the same quarter of the previous year). We have explained our methodology in a previous article. So, ARR retention in the previous quarter was 99.3% compared with 98.7% in 2Q. This was the first increase after three straight quarters of declines. We believe that new AI products will prompt a recovery in retention to above 100% in 2026 (it remains to be seen whether this will fully materialize).

- Adjustments have been made to the target multiple calculation, as more than half of 2025 is now behind us. The calculation now uses expected EBITDA growth in 2026/2025 versus 2025/2024 previously. Growth will be slower in 2026 due to a high base. In calculating the target multiple, we use the base EV/EBITDA multiple multiplied by the EBITDA growth rate for the following year. We calculate the base EV/EBITDA as the average EV/EBITDA of a handful of companies in the industry divided by their EBITDA growth rate by year.

Valuation

We are raising the EBITDA forecast from $315.7 mln (+41% y/y) to $327.4 mln (+45% y/y) for 2025 due to the higher-than-expected actual level in 3Q 2025 and to the recovery of ARR retention. Our forecast for 2026 is $401 mln (+22.5% y/y).

We are lowering the target price from $72 to $67 on account of the changes in the calculation of the target multiple, which went down from 21х to 18.3х). We are maintaining the rating at BUY.

As a reminder, RingCentral's valuation is subject to increased volatility, as the company is in the middle of a transition period and has yet to achieve stable profitability.

Risks for the investment case remain the same as in the initial coverage article:

  • Strong competition. RingCentral operates in a fairly fragmented market, holding a market share of less than 5%. The company's key competitors are Zoom, Microsoft, and Cisco. These are major players with significant resources to increase their own share of the UCaaS market.
  • Volatile financial performance. RingCentral has just come near the operating break-even point. The investment case is built on the assumption that management will continue to optimize operating expenses while ramping up revenue. A reversal of the trend (for example, OPEX starting to rise again as a % of revenue) would result in a significant downward revaluation of the stock.

Bottomline

RingCentral’s stock has lagged the market despite solid operational progress, but the core thesis remains intact: profitability is accelerating, cash flow is strengthening, and ARR retention is stabilizing. AI-driven products are gaining real traction, subscription growth has likely bottomed, and operating leverage is now clearly visible. While near-term revenue guidance softened and valuation remains volatile, rising EBITDA expectations support continued upside potential.

data.title: Southern Copper Is The Best Positioned Copper Pure Play
data.description:
Thesis:

In the galaxy of copper producing stars, Southern Copper Company (SCCO) shines the brightest, and is the best positioned to benefit from the new economy of AI infrastructure, electrification, and renewable energy. Discerning investors should consider jumping onboard!

Company Overview:

The history of the company is one that shows consistency and prudence. Incorporated in the US back in 1952, the company has ridden all the crests and valleys of the commodity price cycles and survived. Its business model is simple – to extract and transform copper and associated minerals to satisfy market demands, fulfill social and environmental requirements, and maximize values for shareholders.

SCCO has remained true to its mission as a copper pure play, and this steadfastness has served the company well. Whereas some of the competition ventured into other lines of the natural resource business, like oil and gas exploration and production, which almost became their Achilles' heels, SCCO has maintained a “steady as she goes” approach, concentrating on what it knows how to do best – extract and transform copper and associated mineral resources into valuable materials efficiently. This singular focus has saved the company from the distractions and inefficiencies that arise from being a ‘jack of trades.’

Copper Demand and Supply Forecasts:

Copper demand is expected to increase to 42.7 million tonnes by 2035 as Copper demand surges. These increases, driven by electrification, renewable energy, and artificial intelligence ((AI) infrastructures, are expected to have major impacts on the prices of the red metal. See figures below for reference:

On the supply side, copper is challenged. From a paucity of large copper deposit discoveries to a lack of new greenfield developments, and generally low investments in the industry, the forecast shows a sustained copper supply deficit that begins this year, 2025, at a 400,000 metric tonnes deficit Supply Falls Behind, and goes on for many years into the future. It follows, therefore, that these conditions present the one-two punch that pushes copper market equilibrium prices ever higher. Please see figures below for reference:

Resource Reserve and (Production) Growth Plans:

SCCO’s copper resource reserves of 51.1 million metric tonnes are the largest in the industry Reserves by Company. This is approximately 16.14% bigger than the next biggest reserve in the industry of 44.0 million tons for Freeport-McMoRan. This large resource base gives SCCO the runway flexibility to grow copper production organically without the need for disadvantageous, expensive acquisitions. And interestingly, the company has mapped out bankable plans to grow its production and hence, its earnings, and cash flows:

In the years between 2022 through 2032, the company would grow production from 895,000 metric tonnes per year to 1,653,000 metric tonnes per year. That would imply almost a doubling (~ 85% growth more precisely) of copper production by the company in this time frame. Please see figures below for reference:

Some of the biggest growth projects would include Tia Maria, Los Chancas, and Michiquillay. No other company has outlined any such plans that would produce such rate of production growth as SCCO in this same time frame. Please see the figure below for reference:

Perhaps, though, what is more telling is where these reserves are located. SCCO’s reserves are located in Mexico and Peru, both in the Americas, both investment grade countries. On the other hand, the crown jewel of FCX, the Grasberg mine, is located in Indonesia. And this country tries its best to squeeze out more and more value from FCX every so often. When they did not reduce the ownership interest of FCX from 90% down to 49%, they restricted the export of copper concentrate by FCX, or increased duties, taxes, and fees on the export of copper concentrate, Indonesia raises taxes. Furthermore, they required FCX to make multi-billion-dollar capital investments like the smelter that, in absolute terms, are not necessary for FCX to conduct its business. The geopolitical risks seem to be unending. Currently, to extend its mining license beyond 2041, FCX has accepted to give up an additional 12% ownership of the Grasberg mine, bringing the company’s ownership interest down to a mere 37% FCX Presentation 3Q2025. Please, see the figure below for reference:

As copper becomes more like the oil of the renewable energy and artificial intelligence era, I am concerned about what other geopolitical risks lurk in the background regarding their overseas operation. This presents a potent risk to FCX's entire business model, cost structure, and valuation. The company's valuation, therefore, must be made to reflect this reality. In contrast, SCCO does not face such issues either in Mexico or in Peru.

Cost Leadership:

SCCO’s shining star status is not only with regard to resource reserves and production growth opportunities, but the company also shows leadership in efficiency of operation. In particular, the company has consistently reported one of the lowest net cash costs of production per pound of copper in the industry. Please see the table below for reference:

Company

Cash Cost per Pound of Copper Net of Credits

Southern Copper Company, SCCO

$0.42

Freeport McMoran, FCX

$1.40

BHP Billiton

I expect that as the many growth projects of the company come online, SCCO will achieve greater economy of scale, leading to even lower costs of production.

Industry Leading Margins:

In the 3rd quarter of 2025, SCCO stronger margins vs the competition. The EBITDA margin for SCCO was 58.5% SCCO Financial Results compared to 33.33% EBITDA margin for FCX Presentation 3Q2025, and 44% for Rio Tinto (RIO) SCCO Presentation 2025 for comparison. The company’s margins is growing both year over year as well as sequentially. Please, see below for reference:

Growing Earnings:

The latest quarterly earnings report remained consistent with the outperformance of SCCO. The company reported quarterly earnings of $1.34 per share, implying annualized EPS of $5.32. For comparison, FCX reported quarterly earnings of $0.46 per share, implying annualized EPS of $1.84.

Valuation:

The hair-splitting valuation modeling (discounted net present value method) I like to use is without the scope of this article. But suffice it to state that presently, SCCO current forward earnings per share stand at $5.32 and that there are two big factors that are sure to drive earnings growth for the company going forward. One of these factors is production increases, and the other is increase in the prices of copper itself. These two powerful trends would drive earnings growth for SCCO in a more parabolic manner.

So, if we assume a doubling of production by SCCO, and a doubling of market prices for copper in the next 10 years, then, holding everything else constant, we can assume that SCCO could earn $21.28 ($5.32*2*2) per share by 2035. And then, applying the same PE multiple as currently obtains (28.73) implies that the price per share of SCCO would be $611.37 ($5.32*2*2*28.73) in 10 years’ time. That would be about 354% return for an investor with such an investment time horizon.

In comparison, if we assume FCX production growth remains unchanged, then, going by current the forward earnings of $1.84 per share, FCX earnings would only benefit from increases (doubling) in prices of copper. If we keep the current PE multiple of 29.48, then FCX prices by 2035 would be $108.49 ($1.84*2*29.48) per share, implying a 152.4% return over the same 10-year investment time horizon.

Conclusion and Trade Suggestion:

No matter how you analyze SCCO – resource reserves, operational efficiency, profitability, etc., the company comes out on top. The only obvious conclusion, therefore, is that the company is the best positioned copper pure play to benefit the most in the new artificial intelligence infrastructure, electrification, and renewable energy era.

For investment and trade suggestions, I would look to buy in the $125 - $130 per share range. This is supported by technical analysis on the price actions chart, and by data science analysis. data.title: Weatherford: Thank You, I'm Out After Q3'25 (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My investment, albeit a small one, in Weatherford International (WFRD) was a bit of a "shot in the dark" according to some of my colleagues whom I spoke to about it. They cited unfavorable fundamentals, they cited junk-rated credit, and they cited a valuation that, according to them, wasn't that great to begin with.

However, since I invested in the company, I have not only beaten the SPY and the market but beaten it handily, by more than a 3x performance since July of this year. That almost 35% RoR in a period of 5 months represents an annualized rate of return of over 65%.

Even if I sold my entire stake today at the time of writing, which would not be a necessarily bad idea given the overvalued state of the market that we're currently in, I would "get away" with a very strong rate of return and positive impacts on my total shareholder return, or TSR. But is it the time to sell Weatherford? Energy has seen a bit of an upswing as of late, and Weatherford's upswing isn't unique in the sector - other oil and gas companies are also up quite a bit, and I've been looking at some of them as candidates for rotation for a few weeks now.

The market valuation gives me pause. Based on monthly data, some analysts are calling the market overvalued (SPY) as high as 198%. You can see that if you look at things like CAPE ratios, but also if you follow reports from world-leading investment and private banks or just straight advisors.

No matter where you look, there's something to be said for this. What I have become a bit "stuck" on is historical data. Specifically, if we look at historical undervaluations, such as the Great Depression, the 1970s, or the 1982 period when it was a good time to buy, this was found during undervaluations of 40-50%. By contrast, if you invested, say, in June of 1932, you'd have found your 10-year nominal TSR at close to 0% or in the negative. If you invested in March of 2009, prior to the GFC, your 10-year nominal return in 2019, close to COVID-19, would have still been negative.

Dear readers, valuation matters. The current levels of valuation approach levels of 1929 or 1965, as well as dot-com bubble valuations. If you invested during that time, your typical 10-year nominal returns would have been weak or negative, and that's the sort of SPY valuation we're moving at today.

That's why I am negative or bearish on the market, and I believe you should be considering it as well.

Let's look at Weatherford International and why I have decided to do what I have done.

Weatherford International - not a great upside here

It's a completely fair characterization to say that Weatherford has mostly recovered much of its undervaluation over the last 7 months. Not all of it - but the company has gone from being a "value story" to a "growth" story. That always shifts things a bit on both the downside and upside - especially in conjunction with a BB rating in credit and a low yield of less than 1.6%.

3Q25 results did not make things much better. EBITDA margin declined by over 3%, even with a slight sequential recovery and upside from better FCF. Despite what I said about some of the oil industry seeing upside, there's still a fair bit of headwind in the sector here. The company's international operations have seen specific impacts from both Saudi Arabia and Mexico, but there are signs that these markets may be about to improve. Mexico, especially, has been a going concern, and I'm surprised the company has been able to rise as much as it has, because Pemex, a major part of the company's sales at over 30% of receivables and 10% of revenues, is not seeing the greatest trends, in that it has used third-party financing to make payments to Weatherford.

As stated, Pemex is also the world's most indebted oil company, so it's definitely worth looking at.

Weatherford has been trying to address its ongoing balance sheet issues. It's been expanding its credit facility and increasing the accordion. It's upsized its offerings for debt restructuring to $1.2B for the 2033 notes, and it looks to further upgrade its credit outlook (after S&P Global's stable outlook at BB). By saving about $30M in interest payments, the company has made moves that are to be applauded. Some success has been seen here, and there is some Weatherford de-risking, even if its customers remain somewhat of an issue.

Growth is so-so. I don't see anything in the near term that would in any way justify a price target increase. The company remains an excessively uncertain play because of the above-mentioned issues tied to Mexico's bills/receivables. How and when these are going to be made remains a concern to me at this time. Furthermore, even if management flagged Mexico for some tangible progress, that doesn't mean the risk is gone.

We also always need to remember that Weatherford is a company that arose from bankruptcy proceedings. This was following several years of losses, shuttered business operations, poor incentive structuring vis-à-vis shareholders, and excessive use of leverage. There's no doubt to me that Weatherford is a more stable business today, but whenever looking at that sort of company, we also want to keep in mind what it "does" as well as what it has achieved.

My main consideration and issue with company operations, by which I mean assigning them any sort of higher multiple or target, is whether they are actually meaningfully differentiated enough to justify a higher valuation. It's not as though there is a lack of oil service companies. The company's targets of 20%+ EBITDA margins are, in my view, unrealistic in the context that no energy service company has managed this in the long run. When it comes to its other operations/solutions, things like drilling, rental, and fishing services, these lack meaningful innovation that could drive upside. Its R&D is also on the low side, and it's comparatively smaller than its peers.

I also cannot help but feel that the market's upside (which, as I said, surprised me) is irrational given the fundamental risk of payment issues and concentration issues to Pemex, one of the world's most (currently the most) indebted oil companies. It also continues to bet on Russian oilfields. Normally, I would respect the company for going against the grain like that, but the problem is that the entirety of Weatherford is starting to look too volatile and too uncertain, versus if we'd seen Russia as more of a "side project".

So based on this, while 3Q25 has some light and positive sides, I consider the totality of the company too impacted here to really allow for a significantly higher valuation.

My approach to the valuation now is therefore as follows.

Valuation for Weatherford International - Pemex and more Pemex

After an almost 35% return, even with credit improvements, the company is no longer undervalued or attractively valued for what it is. My forecasts for the energy sector since July have actually gone lower. This means my fair value for Weatherford, which was at $100/share, is now at $95/share. A slight but meaningful reduction that's also visible in the company's own forecasts, now expecting a 17%+ (negative) return for this year's EPS.

In fact, even if we accept what I would consider to be uncertain forecasts in a field as volatile as energy and allow for a 20% EPS growth 2 years in the future, where there's a Russian invasion of Ukraine still ongoing, the return, even based on that at a 13-14x P/E, would be less than 15% annualized.

My PT is down from $75/share, which would have maintained "BUY" here (if barely), to $70/share. This makes the company, based on straight P/E and forecasts, a "HOLD" and/or a rotation target.

Here things become trickier. Normally, I wouldn't even consider rotating my position here. The difference between my original PT and even the new PT is not high enough. However, with the market in the overvalued state that it currently is (as I stated at the beginning of my article) and with this being a volatile energy company, I have concluded that I am unwilling to bear the risk of losing a 65%+ annualized rate of return at this time.

Therefore, as of yesterday, I have sold my position in Weatherford and increased my overall cash position even more. I am now at more than 40% cash in my portfolio, which is as high as I have been since I started investing. This should tell you about my convictions as they are now.

Remember, I still invest. But I am much more careful and demanding about what I invest in and at what valuation. I also tend to rotate quicker, more - kind of a more "agile" approach if that makes any sense, again reflecting what I view as unfavorable market dynamics and valuation.

Competition in this segment is mainly between two—both much bigger than Weatherford. The big one is SLB Limited (SLB), at over $55B market cap, more than 10x the market cap of Weatherford. It also has a better yield and much better capital allocation. Furthermore, it hasn't been bankrupt. Even putting the company just at the lowest 20-year average that exists, a 14x P/E, SLB has an upside of over 15% annualized here again, with a better yield.

Thus, I call SLB an attractive alternative here. Halliburton is unfortunately also too expensive.

Risks and considerations here are as follows.

Risks and Considerations

The primary risks to my thesis for the company remain peer multiples and appeal, which at this time are quite high. While Weatherford was attractive, it is no longer, and many of the peers are not attractive now either, with the exception of SLB.

The company has come out of C11 a stronger and more defensive business, but this doesn't mean that its risks are gone. The commodity pricing exposure is part of the cyclicality of the business model, and I expect the margins, which are still below the above-mentioned peers, to rise as the company develops, but I doubt they will rise as much as expected by the company.

I would not call this company "speculative", but I would call it overvalued for what it offers and the sector and company volatility here.

Thesis

Weatherford is an international oil/gas service business out of Houston with a domicile in Ireland, hence the PLC. The company has overall solid margins and a good upside if bought at a cheap valuation. That valuation is no longer there, in my view.

I say that the upside for Weatherford is below 15% annualized, even if we expect a relatively high fair value of $95/share, downgraded from my latest article for the 2027E period, making it a price target of around $70/share. The upside of 15% annualized is no longer there.

As of the 3Q25 period, I say this company represents a 'Hold' with an unclear upside, potentially above 10% but probably below 15% - but I also remind you that peers are in fact even more fundamentally appealing here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is not enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a price target of $70/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a 'Hold' rating as of December 2025.

data.title: Tracking The Great Value Shift In The Global Equity Landscape
data.description:
By Avi Lavi

Value stocks have bounced back with gusto outside the US. But can it last?

While the AI-driven rally in US mega-cap growth stocks grabbed attention in 2025, a very different story was unfolding far from Wall Street. Outside the US, from Europe to Japan, value stocks shed their perennial underdog status to stage a dramatic recovery—one that we think may just be getting started.

Global equity markets were full of surprises this year. Japanese and European stocks outpaced US peers. The MSCI EAFE Value Index of non-US equities surged by 36.5% through November (Display) in US-dollar terms, outperforming the MSCI EAFE Growth and the broad index. It was a mirror image of the US market, where growth stocks remained on top.

Strong Revival: Value Stocks Outside the US Have Had a Stellar Year

What’s Fueled the Value Recovery?

Several catalysts helped awaken value stocks, which began the year with exceptionally cheap valuations: first, companies in the value universe generally delivered earnings growth above expectations. Second, key value-oriented industries performed especially well, including European banks—as a steep yield curve buoyed profitability—and defense. Third, Asian corporate governance reforms are gaining traction.

In Europe, regional defense spending has increased amid heightened geopolitical tensions related to the Russia-Ukraine war and uncertainty over the US defense umbrella. EU defense spending rose by 19% in 2024 to €343 billion, or 1.9% of GDP, according to the European Council. The North Atlantic Treaty Organization is pressing its members to allocate at least 3% of GDP for defense budgets over the coming years, which could add another €220 billion a year to European military spending.

That translates to a big business boost for large, regional defense contractors, which can support stable cash flows and dividend payouts. These companies are prominent members of the value cohort.

Asia’s Corporate Governance Catalyst

Meanwhile, a new culture of corporate governance reform is taking root across Asia. China, South Korea and Japan are pushing reforms to boost shareholder value. In Japan, regulators are encouraging companies to reduce cross-shareholdings and deploy hoarded cash to boost operating leverage and streamline balance sheets. These moves could prompt a recovery in select Japanese companies with attractive valuations and a clear path to improve profitability.

China, too, is pressing companies to improve governance via the 2024 nine-point guidelines. These moves have helped lift profitability, dividends and buybacks to record highs. South Korean initiatives are in full swing through the “value up” program, which aims to incentivize improved capital management and operating performance. The goal: to boost valuations in a South Korean market that has had an exceptionally high proportion of cheaply valued stocks for years.

Is There More Room to Run?

After such a strong year, it’s natural for investors to question the staying power of non-US value stocks. We think there are good reasons to believe that the trend can continue.

First, the catalysts above are long-term trends that should continue to evolve. Defense spending in Europe is being driven by seismic geopolitical shifts that aren’t going away. Corporate governance reform is a multi-year process; while policy-driven changes can be erratic, we expect developments in Asia to create a virtuous circle, as more companies discover the benefits of cleaning up their businesses for shareholders.

Second, we’re seeing signs of other value-friendly themes developing. Examples include rising demand for commercial aircraft, a recovery in depressed agricultural commodity prices and the recovery of healthcare R&D spending from post-pandemic pressures. These trends support select opportunities in value-oriented companies with healthier businesses than perceived.

Third, new forces could propel value market segments that haven’t yet rebounded. For example, a continued weakening of the US dollar could support emerging-market stocks, which have not yet fully participated in the value rally.

Valuations Are Still Attractive

Even after this year’s rebound, ex-US value stocks still offer attractive discounts for investors by several measures. The MSCI EAFE Value’s price-to-forward-earnings ratio trades at a discount of 43% to growth stocks and 21% to the broader market (Display).

Value Remains Priced at Near Record Discount, Even After Recent Outperformance

Free cash flow (FCF) yields also tell a compelling value story. We believe the FCF yield is an important indicator of whether a company is undervalued or overvalued based on its cash-generating ability. Our research shows a widening gap between FCF yields and government bond yields in the US versus Europe and Japan, indicating stronger compensation potential for risk-taking and better value opportunities. US equities have a negative real FCF yield spread versus Treasuries, signaling stretched valuations.

As we see it, the FCF yield analysis bolsters the case for allocating to value equities outside the US, particularly at a time of elevated market concentration in the US and increasing questions about the fragility of AI-driven market conditions.

Markets are fraught with risk as we enter 2026. That’s why a selective and risk-aware approach remains paramount. We think investors should search for value opportunities in three areas: companies undergoing positive changes, businesses with underappreciated sustainable competitive advantages and firms that benefit from improving industry competitive dynamics.

After years of unforgiving market conditions, disciplined value investing is starting to pay off. It’s not too late to diversify your equity allocation by increasing exposure to value stocks around the world with compelling recovery stories that represent powerful return potential.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio management teams and are subject to change over time.

MSCI makes no express or implied warranties or representations, and shall have no liability whatsoever with respect to any MSCI data contained herein.

The MSCI data may not be further redistributed or used as a basis for other indices or any securities or financial products. This report is not approved, reviewed or produced by MSCI.
